planting.note: Plant in spring as a specimen or informal hedge.

directions: Plant in full to partial sun in acidic, well-drained soil and water deeply until established. New growth emerges bright reddish-purple and matures to a rich burgundy. Bright pink tasseled flowers cover the shrub in spring with lighter flushes later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a mixed border or foundation planting.

directions: Set into acidic soil at the same depth it grew in the pot and mulch well. New leaves flush bright ruby-red before settling to deep purple with age. Hot-pink fringe flowers appear in profusion in spring and repeat lightly through warm weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact rounded accent shrub.

directions: Choose a sunny, well-drained spot with acidic soil and plant level with the ground. This slower, denser selection holds intense purple foliage all year and needs little pruning to stay tidy. Bright pink fringe blooms cover the plant in spring with repeat flushes into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in the ground, in a container, or spilling over a wall.

directions: Use this low, weeping selection as a groundcover or trailing container plant in acidic, well-drained soil. Its cascading branches stay under two feet tall while spreading several feet wide, needing almost no pruning. Purple leaves and pink fringe flowers combine for spring color that repeats through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact mounding shrub for small spaces.

directions: Plant this naturally dwarf, mounding form in acidic soil where it will stay knee-high without shearing. Give it full sun for the richest purple foliage and steady moisture while young. Deep pink fringe flowers cover the compact mound in spring with lighter reblooming later.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, spreading foundation shrub.

directions: Site this low, wider-than-tall selection in full sun and acidic, well-drained soil. It holds vivid red-burgundy foliage through the year and stays compact with little pruning. Bright red fringe flowers blanket the plant in spring and flush again during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a green-leaved, white-flowered accent.

directions: Plant in acidic soil in full sun to light shade and keep evenly moist while establishing. Unlike the purple-leaved kinds, this selection keeps emerald-green foliage as a backdrop. Clean white fringe flowers cover the rounded shrub in spring with occasional rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring where dark foliage and red bloom will show off.

directions: Give this selection full sun and acidic, well-drained soil for the darkest near-black foliage. It grows into a dense mounded shrub and needs only light shaping after bloom. Deep red fringe flowers contrast strongly against the black-purple leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a mid-size shrub or informal hedge.

directions: Plant in full sun to part shade in acidic soil and mulch to conserve moisture. This selection carries deep purple leaves and blooms heavily rather than sporadically. Bright ruby-pink fringe flowers appear in spring and rebloom through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact rounded shrub.

directions: Set this compact, well-branched selection into acidic, well-drained soil in a sunny spot. Its purple-green foliage forms a tidy mound that rarely needs pruning. Soft pink fringe flowers cover the plant in spring with reliable rebloom later on.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a large upright shrub is wanted.

directions: Give this vigorous, large selection room to reach shrub or small-tree size in acidic soil. Plant in full sun for strongest purple foliage and water deeply during dry spells. Big pink fringe flowers open in spring across a tall, upright frame.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright light to keep the leaf variegation crisp.

directions: Plant in acidic soil with morning sun and light afternoon shade to protect the variegated leaves. The foliage is splashed cream, pink, and green on a compact frame that needs little care. Pink fringe flowers appear in spring against the multicolored leaves.

common_name: Jazz Hands Dwarf White Fringe Flower

planting.note: Plant in spring as a very compact green-and-white accent.

directions: Use this tiny selection at the front of a bed or in containers in acidic, well-drained soil. It keeps green foliage and stays under two feet with essentially no pruning. White fringe flowers dot the low mound in spring.

common_name: Jazz Hands Dwarf Pink Fringe Flower

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low mounding shrub for tight spaces.

directions: Plant this dwarf, purple-leaved form in acidic soil in full sun for best color. It forms a dense low mound that fits edging, borders, and pots without shearing. Pink fringe flowers cover the plant in spring with scattered rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a large dark-leaved shrub is desired.

directions: Give this large selection full sun and acidic soil for the deepest near-black foliage. It grows into a substantial rounded shrub and takes hard pruning well if size must be controlled. Hot-pink fringe flowers stand out against the dark leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a green-leaved white-flowering shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic soil in full sun to part shade and keep watered while establishing. This green-foliaged selection makes a large rounded shrub useful as a screen. Soft creamy-white fringe flowers cover the plant in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading mid-size shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade and mulch to hold moisture. The olive-purple foliage sets off vivid raspberry-pink blooms. Fringe flowers appear heavily in spring with lighter flushes through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a medium upright shrub.

directions: Site in full sun in acidic soil and water regularly during the first year. This selection carries deep burgundy leaves and stands out for its red-toned fringe flowers. Bloom peaks in spring with repeat color into summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact garden shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in full to partial sun and keep evenly moist while young. Its reddish-purple new growth matures to bronzy green. Deep pink fringe flowers cover the shrub in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a green-and-white flowering shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic soil in full sun to part shade and water in well. This green-leaved selection makes a rounded shrub covered in white spring bloom. Clean white fringe flowers open in early spring against fresh green foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a colorful mid-size shrub.

directions: Give this selection full sun and acidic, well-drained soil for the richest leaf color. Burgundy-red new foliage tops the arching branches through the season. Vivid pink-red fringe flowers cover the plant heavily in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact rounded shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in full to partial sun and mulch to retain moisture. This more compact selection stays fairly low with purple-tinged foliage. Pink fringe flowers appear in spring with scattered later bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a burgundy-leaved specimen shrub.

directions: Site in full sun in acidic soil so the plum-purple foliage holds its color. It grows into a large arching shrub that responds well to renewal pruning after bloom. Bright pink fringe flowers cover the branches in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a rounded flowering shrub.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade and water deeply while establishing. The reddish new growth matures to green-bronze on a vigorous rounded shrub. Rose-pink fringe flowers appear heavily in spring and rebloom into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a large landscape shrub.

directions: One of the earliest red-leaf selections in the trade, plant in acidic soil in full sun for best color. Give it room, as it becomes a large arching shrub over time. Deep pink fringe flowers cover the plant in spring against burgundy foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact dark-leaved shrub.

directions: Plant this compact selection in acidic, well-drained soil in full sun for the darkest foliage. It holds deep purple-black leaves and stays fairly tidy with light pruning. Bright pink fringe flowers cover the mound in spring with rebloom in flushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low green-leaved mounding shrub.

directions: Use this dwarf, green-leaved form as a low mound in acidic, well-drained soil. It stays compact and needs little pruning to hold its rounded shape. White fringe flowers dot the plant in spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once hard frost has passed and the soil has begun to warm.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing about a quarter inch deep in fast-draining soil. Give it a hot, dry, sunny spot and prune lightly after flowering to keep the rush-like stems dense. Expect fragrant golden pea-flowers from early summer into autumn once the plant is established.

planting.note: Plant in spring into lean, sharply drained ground in full sun.

directions: Scarify the seed and sow a quarter inch deep, or set out container plants in spring since brooms resent root disturbance. Avoid rich soil and heavy feeding, which produce soft growth, and shear only lightly after bloom to avoid cutting into old wood. Golden-yellow flowers smother the green stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Set out a rooted plant in spring while it is settling into new growth.

directions: Grow from semi-ripe summer cuttings or buy a young plant, since this named form does not come true from seed. Plant in poor, well-drained soil and full sun, and prune lightly right after flowering to keep it bushy. The bicolored yellow-and-crimson pea flowers open along arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant a container-grown shrub in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Propagate from cuttings or plant a young nursery shrub, as hybrid brooms will not reproduce true from seed. Site it in full sun and lean soil, and shear the flowered shoots back lightly, never into bare old wood. Rich cerise-red flowers edged with gold cover the plant in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Set out a young plant in spring in a sunny, freely draining bed.

directions: Buy a rooted plant or take summer cuttings, since this compact selection must be grown vegetatively. Keep it in poor soil and bright sun, trimming lightly after bloom to hold its tidy shape. Two-tone flowers of ruby-red and buttery yellow crowd the stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and gritty, well-drained ground.

directions: Increase from cuttings or plant a nursery shrub, as the deep color will not carry through from seed. Give it lean soil and unshaded sun, and prune the flowered wood back lightly once bloom fades. Deep ruby-red flowers cover the arching branches in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Set out a young plant in spring in a hot, sunny position.

directions: Propagate by summer cuttings or plant a container-grown specimen for true color. Poor, sharply drained soil and full sun suit it best, with only light trimming after flowering. Vivid red flowers clothe the slender green stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and freely draining soil.

directions: Raise from cuttings or plant a young shrub, since named brooms are not seed-propagated. Set it in lean soil and full sun and prune lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Bright golden-yellow flowers smother the upright stems in late spring.

planting.note: Set out a container plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a nursery shrub, as this hybrid does not come true from seed. Give it poor soil and full sun, and shear the arching stems lightly right after bloom. Masses of creamy pale-yellow flowers cascade over the mounded plant in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and gritty, freely draining soil.

directions: Propagate from summer cuttings or buy a young plant to keep the color true. Lean soil and full sun bring the best flowering, with only light pruning after the show. Long-lasting deep golden-yellow flowers drench the arching branches in mid spring.

planting.note: Set out a young shrub in spring into a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Increase by cuttings or plant a container specimen, since the hybrid will not seed true. Poor, well-drained soil in full sun suits it, and it needs only a light trim after bloom. Bicolored flowers of cream and rosy cerise-pink cover the plant in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the top of a wall or bank where the stems can trail.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a young shrub, as this low hybrid is propagated vegetatively. Site it in full sun with fast drainage and let the prostrate branches spill over a ledge or slope. Pale creamy-yellow flowers wreathe the spreading stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a bed or over a wall in full sun.

directions: Scarify the seed and sow shallowly, or plant a young container shrub since it dislikes being moved. Give it sharp drainage and full sun on a slope or raised edge where the arching stems can cascade. It becomes a mound of bright golden-yellow flowers in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a lean, sunny site.

directions: Nick the hard seed and sow a quarter inch deep, or set out a small plant in spring. Poor, dry, well-drained soil in full sun keeps it compact, and a light shear after bloom encourages a tidier habit. Spikes of yellow pea flowers appear from early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant a young shrub in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Raise from cuttings or plant a nursery specimen to keep the selection true. Set it in lean, dry soil and full sun and trim lightly after flowering. Abundant deep golden-yellow flowers cover the neat mound through early and mid summer.

planting.note: Set out a young plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a container shrub, as the double form is sterile and must be cloned. Give it dry, poor soil and full sun over a wall or bank where the low stems can spread. Long-blooming double golden flowers wreathe the prostrate branches through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered, sunny position with sharp drainage.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young tree since it grows quickly. Give it lean, freely draining soil and full sun, and prune only lightly when young to shape the airy, weeping crown. Fragrant golden flowers cover the near-leafless green branches in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where its spiny mound can form a low barrier.

directions: Scarify the seed and sow shallowly, or plant a young shrub in spring. Poor, dry, well-drained soil in full sun keeps the prickly cushion dense and floriferous. Small golden pea flowers smother the spiny mound in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant a young shrub in spring over a wall or bank in full sun.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a container specimen for the true selection. Give it lean, sharply drained soil and full sun where the flat, trailing stems can spread. A carpet of bright golden-yellow flowers covers the mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a bed or in a trough in full sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young mat-forming shrub. Its curious winged green stems spread flat over gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Clusters of yellow pea flowers stand above the ribbon-like stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm, sheltered, sunny wall in mild areas.

directions: Soak the seed and start it warm indoors, then plant out young shrubs in a protected spot. Fast draining soil, full sun, and shelter from cold wind suit its silvery, laburnum-like leaves. Cone-shaped clusters of yellow flowers smelling of pineapple open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall where it is protected from hard frost.

directions: Propagate from cuttings or plant a young grafted shrub to keep this large-flowered form true. Give it a sheltered sunny wall and sharp drainage, pruning only to shape after bloom. Big pineapple-scented cones of yellow flowers appear against the silvery foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and lean, sharply drained soil.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young container shrub in spring. Poor soil and full sun suit this tall, upright broom, and it needs only light pruning after bloom. Countless small white pea flowers cover the slender stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring over a low wall or bank in full sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young low shrub in spring. Give it sharp drainage and full sun where the semi-prostrate stems can spread. Unusual lilac to purple pea flowers line the arching branches in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or over a ledge in full sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young mat-forming shrub. It hugs gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and needs no regular pruning. Bright yellow flowers cover the flat, ground-hugging stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and freely draining soil.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young shrub in spring. Lean soil and full sun keep it upright and floriferous, and it can be cut back in spring since it blooms on new wood. Slender upright spikes of yellow flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild zones or grow in a container to shelter over winter.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a young shrub, as this florist's hybrid is propagated vegetatively. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, and in cold regions keep it potted to bring under cover before frost. Fragrant golden-yellow flowers cover the arching stems in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or trough in full sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young alpine shrub. This dwarf broom needs gritty, sharply drained soil and full sun in a raised bed. Golden-yellow flowers cover the tiny cushion in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and poor, well-drained soil.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a small shrub in spring. This spiny low broom thrives on lean, dry soil in full sun and needs little care. Short spikes of yellow flowers open in early summer above the slightly thorny stems.

planting.note: Plant a young shrub in spring over a bank or wall in full sun.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a container specimen to keep the selection true. Lean, gritty soil and full sun suit its low, spreading stems on a slope. A dense sheet of bright yellow flowers covers the mat in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Set out a young plant in spring in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Propagate from cuttings or plant a nursery shrub, as this hybrid will not seed true. Poor soil and full sun bring the heaviest bloom, with only light shearing after flowering. Pure white flowers smother the arching mound in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and freely draining soil.

directions: Raise from cuttings or plant a young shrub, since named brooms are not seed-grown. Set it in lean soil and full sun and trim lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Soft pale sulphur-yellow flowers cover the stems in late spring.

planting.note: Set out a young plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Grow from cuttings or plant a container shrub for the true color. Give it poor soil and full sun, with a light trim of flowered shoots afterward. Bicolored flowers blending lilac-pink and cream cover the arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rock garden or raised bed in full sun.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed shallowly, or plant a young shrub in spring. This tidy dome of rush-like green stems needs sharp drainage and full sun. Small yellow flowers dot the neat mound in early summer.

planting.note: Set out potted plants in spring once hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant with the crown about two inches below the soil surface and give this vigorous grower a large fence, pergola, or tree to scramble through. It flowers on the previous year's wood, so prune only after the spring flush to shape and control size. Expect a cloud of soft pink, vanilla-scented blooms in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot protected from cold drying winds.

directions: Site this broadleaf evergreen against a warm sheltered wall and bury the crown a couple of inches deep. Prune lightly right after flowering to remove tangled or winter-damaged stems, since blooms form on old wood. Clusters of fragrant white star-shaped flowers open in early spring above glossy leathery leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species tolerates cold exposed sites well.

directions: Set the crown two inches below the surface and let it climb a trellis, low wall, or shrub. Being an early bloomer on old wood, it needs no regular pruning beyond tidying after the flowers fade. Nodding bell-shaped blue flowers appear in mid-spring, followed by fluffy silvery seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hardy position; very cold tolerant once established.

directions: Plant with the base slightly deep and train it up an obelisk or open trellis where its dainty flowers can dangle freely. It blooms on last season's growth, so simply trim lightly after flowering if needed. Semi-double lavender-blue lantern-like flowers open in spring and give way to feathery seed clusters.

planting.note: Plant in spring; blooms the same summer on new growth.

directions: Set the crown a few inches deep and provide a fence or shrub host to weave through. Because it flowers on the current year's stems, cut the whole plant back to about a foot in late winter. Masses of small nodding violet-purple flowers cover the vine from midsummer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates poor lean soils and cold winters.

directions: Plant the crown a couple of inches deep and let it ramble over a sturdy fence or bank. Hard prune in late winter since bloom comes on new wood. Nodding lantern-shaped yellow flowers appear all summer and are joined by silky spherical seed heads that persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm sunny spot with good drainage.

directions: Plant the crown a couple of inches below grade and offer a trellis or shrub for support. Prune hard in late winter because flowering occurs on the season's new shoots. Small urn-shaped scarlet-red flowers nod along the stems from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a sheltered warm wall in mild-winter regions.

directions: Site this winter-flowering evergreen against a sunny sheltered wall and set the crown slightly deep. Prune only lightly after flowering to untangle stems, since blooms come on older wood. Cream bell-shaped flowers, often freckled maroon inside, open through late fall and winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a non-climbing herbaceous type that needs light staking.

directions: Plant at the same depth as the pot in a border and give the lax stems a low support to lean on. Cut the whole plant to the ground in late winter, as it flowers on new growth. Nodding bell-shaped blue flowers appear through summer, followed by twisted silvery seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest scent.

directions: Plant the crown a couple of inches deep and provide a fence or large shrub to clamber over. Prune hard in late winter since it blooms on new wood. Airy sprays of small almond-scented white flowers open in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the crown set deep to encourage strong shoots.

directions: Bury the crown about three inches deep to protect against clematis wilt and mulch the roots to keep them cool. This is a new-wood bloomer, so cut all stems back to about a foot in late winter. Large velvety deep-purple flowers smother the vine from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade so the pale flowers keep their color.

directions: Set the crown a few inches deep and site it out of hot afternoon sun, which fades the delicate coloring. Prune only lightly in early spring, removing dead tips, since it flowers on both old and new wood. Large pale mauve flowers with a rosy central bar appear in early summer with a lighter repeat in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the crown set deep and roots shaded.

directions: Plant the crown deep and keep the root zone cool with mulch or low companion plants. Prune lightly in early spring by tidying dead growth, as blooms form on old and new wood. Rich purple-blue flowers with silvery undersides open in early summer and repeat later in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates some shade but flowers best in good light.

directions: Set the crown several inches below grade and shade the roots while letting the top climb into sun. Give it a light early-spring prune since it blooms on both old and new wood. Large creamy-white flowers with dark stamens appear in early summer and again in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the crown deep; a reliable new-wood bloomer.

directions: Plant the crown about three inches deep and mulch to keep the roots cool. Cut the plant back hard in late winter since flowers come on the current year's stems. Glowing carmine-red flowers with golden stamens bloom from summer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; free-flowering and easy for beginners.

directions: Set the crown deep and keep the base shaded with mulch or nearby plants. Prune hard in late winter as it blooms on new growth. Rounded rosy-pink flowers with gently ruffled edges cover the vine through midsummer and into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the richest color.

directions: Plant the crown a few inches deep and give it a sunny wall or trellis. It flowers best when pruned hard in late winter to encourage strong new shoots. Vivid magenta-red flowers with a velvety sheen open from midsummer into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring with the crown deep; a compact popular variety.

directions: Bury the crown deep and shade the roots while training the stems into good light. Give it a light early-spring prune since it flowers on old and new wood. Deep ruby-red almost black flowers with yellow stamens open in early summer and repeat into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a double-flowered sport well suited to containers.

directions: Set the crown deep and grow it up an obelisk or large pot with a support. Prune lightly in early spring, removing only dead stems, as it flowers on old and new wood. Distinctive double blue-purple flowers with a spiky pompom center appear through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade to preserve the bright bar color.

directions: Plant the crown a few inches deep and keep the roots cool and shaded. Give it a light tidy in early spring since blooms come on both old and new wood. Large rose-pink flowers with a deeper carmine central bar open in late spring and repeat in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; vigorous and adaptable to sun or light shade.

directions: Set the crown deep and mulch the base to keep the root run cool. Prune lightly in early spring, as it blooms on old and new wood. Broad mid-blue flowers with slightly wavy edges and creamy stamens appear in early summer and again later.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade so the soft pink does not bleach.

directions: Plant the crown deep and place it where hot midday sun will not fade the flowers. Cut back hard in late winter since it blooms on new wood. Shell-pink flowers with contrasting dark stamens cover this compact vine from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; also sold as 'Madame Le Coultre'.

directions: Set the crown several inches deep and keep the roots cool while the top reaches sun. Give it a light early-spring prune, as flowers form on old and new wood. Large rounded pure-white flowers with overlapping sepals bloom from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact Estonian variety ideal for pots.

directions: Plant the crown deep and grow this short variety up a container obelisk or low trellis. Trim lightly in early spring, as it flowers on old and new wood. Early blooms can be semi-double while later ones are single, all pale pink with a deeper central band.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partial shade to keep the cream tones.

directions: Set the crown deep and site it out of strong sun, which turns the flowers nearly white. Prune lightly in early spring since it blooms on old and new wood. Creamy-yellow flowers with a soft green midrib open in late spring on this compact vine.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a robust Polish variety with deep color.

directions: Plant the crown deep and mulch the roots to keep them cool in summer heat. Cut back hard in late winter since it flowers on new wood. Velvety royal-purple flowers with golden stamens bloom from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tough viticella type resistant to wilt.

directions: Set the crown a few inches deep and let it scramble through a shrub, fence, or rose. Cut it back hard in late winter as it blooms on new growth. Small nodding deep-purple flowers with yellow centers appear in profusion from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; vigorous and very free-flowering.

directions: Plant the crown deep and give it a large host such as a fence or wall shrub. Prune hard in late winter since flowering is on new wood. Rich violet-purple flowers with dark red anthers cover the vine from mid summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; lightly fragrant and long blooming.

directions: Set the crown a couple of inches deep and let the slender stems weave through a trellis or shrub. Cut back hard in late winter as it blooms on new wood. Nodding lavender-blue bell-shaped flowers with recurved tips appear all summer and carry a light scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic fully double white variety.

directions: Plant the crown deep and shade the roots to encourage the best doubles. Prune lightly in early spring, as the double flowers form on old wood and later singles on new wood. Rosette-like fully double white flowers open in early summer, sometimes with a green tint.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous much-loved sky-blue variety.

directions: Set the crown deep and mulch to keep the roots cool while the top climbs into full sun. Prune hard in late winter since it blooms on new wood. Abundant azure-blue flowers with a faint pink flush open from midsummer well into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact and floriferous, good for containers.

directions: Plant the crown deep and support this restrained grower on a trellis or obelisk. Cut back hard in late winter as flowering is on new wood. Masses of mid-blue flowers with slightly textured sepals appear from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the longest-blooming large-flowered types.

directions: Set the crown several inches deep and keep the roots shaded and cool. Give it a light early-spring prune since blooms come on old and new wood. Large lavender-blue flowers with well-spaced sepals appear from late spring through much of summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest velvety color.

directions: Plant the crown deep and mulch to keep the root zone cool. Cut back hard in late winter since it flowers on new wood. Large velvety violet-purple flowers with a warm reddish tone bloom from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact and early flowering.

directions: Set the crown deep and grow this tidy variety up an obelisk or against a wall. Prune lightly in early spring, as it flowers on old and new wood. Rounded white flowers with striking reddish-brown stamens open in late spring with a lighter repeat later.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a vigorous scented montana selection.

directions: Give this large grower a substantial fence, arch, or tree and plant the crown slightly deep. Prune only after flowering, since blooms form on the previous year's wood. Soft pink vanilla-scented flowers smother the vine in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dependable free-flowering blue variety.

directions: Plant the crown deep and shade the roots while training the top toward the light. Prune lightly in early spring since flowers come on old and new wood. Large clear Wedgwood-blue flowers with cream stamens open in late spring and repeat in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it can climb a fence or arbor in full sun.

directions: Give this heavy-blooming selection a warm sunny wall and a support to twine through. The bright orange-red flowers open over a long season and pull in hummingbirds. Cut back leggy stems after the first flush to keep growth tidy and flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in full to part sun.

directions: This yellow-flowered form of native coral honeysuckle needs decent drainage and a support to climb. Set it out in spring and water until established, then expect clear yellow trumpets from late spring into autumn. Shear spent flowers to prompt repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a fence or pergola in full sun.

directions: Train this late-flowering selection onto a strong structure and keep the roots mulched. Its rich scarlet trumpets appear a little later than most and continue into fall. Trim after the main show to keep the vine well furnished with leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it can twine up a trellis in sun.

directions: Set this soft-yellow form out in spring and provide a support for its twining stems. Full sun brings the most flowers, which open through the warm months. Remove faded clusters to encourage a second round of bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis or arbor in full sun.

directions: Give this deep-red selection a sunny spot with room to climb and mulch to keep the roots cool. The intense crimson trumpets flower over a long stretch of the growing season. Cut back after flowering slows to tidy and refresh the vine.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall with roots shaded and stems in the light.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and keep the base cool with mulch or low plantings while the top reaches for sun. The fragrant cream-and-pink flowers are strongest in the evening and give way to red berries. Thin out old wood after flowering to keep the tangle open.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall against a wall or fence in dappled sun.

directions: Set this classic late-flowering form where its roots stay shaded and its top reaches good light. Deep red-purple buds open to fragrant cream flowers from midsummer into autumn. Prune the previous year's shoots back after bloom to encourage fresh flowering wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall on a support in part sun.

directions: Plant this early-blooming Dutch form with the base kept cool and shaded. Reddish buds open to fragrant cream flowers in early summer, sometimes with a lighter second flush. Trim lightly after the first bloom to keep it neat.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall against an arbor in dappled light.

directions: Give this long-flowering selection a cool root run and a frame to scramble over. Its fragrant white flowers age to a warm yellow and appear over many weeks in summer. Prune out a portion of old stems each year after flowering to renew it.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in full to part sun.

directions: Chosen for exceptional fragrance, this selection wants a support and a cool root zone. Yellow-and-white flowers scent the garden through summer and are followed by red berries. Deadhead and thin old growth to keep it flowering freely.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall on a wall or fence in part sun.

directions: Plant this fragrant form with roots shaded and stems trained to the light. Pink-flushed buds open to pale cream flowers in early to midsummer. Cut back some of the flowered wood afterward to keep new growth coming.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a sturdy support in full sun.

directions: Give this fragrant selection full sun and something to climb for the best flowering. Pink-and-gold blooms draw hummingbirds and butterflies over a long season. Shear lightly after the main flush and prune to shape in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a trellis in full to part sun.

directions: Set this vigorous hybrid where it can twine and keep its roots shaded. Orange-red buds open to yellow-throated flowers over a long summer season. Prune in late winter and remove tired stems to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a fence or trellis in full sun; very cold hardy.

directions: This exceptionally hardy hybrid thrives in cold gardens with full sun and a support. Bright scarlet trumpets bloom from early summer until frost and are a hummingbird magnet. Prune in early spring and shear spent flowers to prolong the display.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in full sun.

directions: Grow this scarlet-flowered hybrid in full sun with a frame to climb. The tubular orange-red flowers attract hummingbirds through the warm months. Prune in early spring to control size and encourage flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its vigorous growth can be controlled.

directions: This fast, fragrant selection makes good cover on fences and banks but needs firm management to stay in bounds. White flowers fade to yellow and perfume the air all summer. Shear or cut back hard whenever it outgrows its space.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a support where it can be contained.

directions: Grown for its purple-flushed foliage, this vigorous vine wants a support and regular pruning. The fragrant flowers are purple-red outside and cream within, opening through summer. Keep it trimmed to control its enthusiastic growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in full to part sun.

directions: This form is prized for leaves netted in bright yellow veining, most vivid in good light. It flowers less freely than the type but the foliage carries the season. Give it a support and trim to keep it dense and colorful.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its spreading habit can be managed.

directions: This purple-tinged variety twines readily and roots along the ground, so keep it away from unmanaged areas. Its fragrant flowers open cream and are backed by purplish new growth. Cut back regularly to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring with roots shaded and top in dappled light.

directions: This showy hybrid prefers a cool, part-shaded root run and gentle light on its top growth. Clusters of coppery yellow-orange trumpets appear in early summer, though they carry little scent. Thin out old wood after flowering to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a support in sun to part shade.

directions: Valued for its glossy semi-evergreen leaves, this vigorous vine gives good year-round cover in milder zones. Small red-and-yellow flowers in summer are followed by dark berries. Provide a strong support and prune after flowering to control size.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall with roots shaded and top in the sun.

directions: This fragrant species carries its flowers above fused, cup-like leaves at the stem tips. Cream flowers flushed pink open in early summer and scent the evening air, followed by orange berries. Keep the roots cool and thin old growth after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall against a wall in part sun.

directions: This fragrant hybrid twines readily and blooms freely with a cool root run. Pink-and-cream flowers open in early summer and often repeat later in the season. Give it a support and prune out old wood after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a low trellis in sun to part shade.

directions: This compact native twiner works well on smaller supports and at woodland edges. Yellow-green to purplish flowers open in late spring above fused terminal leaves, followed by red berries. It tolerates cold well and needs only light pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring at a woodland edge with roots shaded.

directions: This western native climbs through shrubs and trees at forest margins and likes a cool, shaded root run. Whorls of orange trumpet flowers open in late spring and pull in hummingbirds, followed by translucent red berries. Give it a support and prune only to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny spot with shelter in colder zones.

directions: This Mediterranean species enjoys heat, sun, and good drainage and tolerates dry conditions once established. Fragrant cream flowers ageing to yellow appear in summer, sometimes flushed pink. Shelter it from cold winds in marginal areas and prune after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered, sunny position.

directions: This free-flowering selection wants heat, sun, and sharp drainage. Large heads of fragrant cream-to-yellow flowers open through summer and are a magnet for pollinators. Give it a warm wall in cooler climates and trim after the display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny, well-drained site.

directions: This evergreen Mediterranean species suits hot, dry gardens with excellent drainage. Fragrant cream-and-pink flowers open in early summer above blue-green fused leaves. It dislikes cold, wet soils, so plant it warm and prune only lightly.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free gardens on a strong support.

directions: The largest honeysuckle, this tender giant needs a warm, frost-free climate and a very sturdy structure to support its heavy growth. Long tubular flowers open cream and age to deep orange-yellow, releasing rich evening fragrance. Feed and water well in the growing season and prune to keep it manageable.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a warm wall with roots kept cool.

directions: This semi-evergreen Chinese climber is grown for its slender, very fragrant flowers. White blooms ageing to soft yellow open over a long summer season above dark leaves. Give it a sheltered spot, a cool root run, and prune after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a trellis in full sun; good in cold climates.

directions: This cold-hardy hybrid group of coral and hairy honeysuckle is grown for its scarlet trumpet flowers. Blooms appear in early summer and often repeat, drawing hummingbirds. Give it full sun and a support, and prune in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered, sunny position.

directions: This Spanish species is grown for glaucous blue-green foliage and fragrant flowers. Reddish buds open to cream-and-pink blooms in summer above the striking leaves. It needs warmth, sun, and sharp drainage, so shelter it in cooler gardens.

planting.note: Plant dormant against a strong wall or pergola in spring.

directions: Settle the crown at soil level and train the main stems along a durable frame. The short, plump racemes are wrapped in fine silvery hairs and carry a strong sweet fragrance. Prune whippy summer growth back hard, then tidy again in winter; flowers open with the new leaves in late spring.

planting.note: A Chinese-by-Japanese hybrid; plant dormant on a heavy support.

directions: Plant grafted or cutting-grown stock at pot depth and anchor it to a strong structure. This hybrid combines the vigor of Chinese wisteria with fuller racemes, so summer and winter spur pruning are both important. Violet-blue trusses open in late spring, often with all the flowers on a truss opening nearly together.

planting.note: Grow over a high pergola so the very long trusses hang clear.

directions: Train this selection along an overhead beam so its exceptionally long racemes, which can trail a foot or more, drape freely beneath. Plant at nursery depth and prune to open spurs in summer and winter. Cascades of reddish-violet flowers open in late spring for a dramatic curtain of bloom.

planting.note: Plant dormant on a strong frame in spring.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and tie stems to a sturdy support as they extend. Cut back leggy shoots in midsummer and shorten spurs again in winter to build flowering wood. Long trusses of soft pale-pink flowers tipped with lilac open with the leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Also sold as 'Alba'; plant dormant on a heavy support.

directions: Plant at pot depth and guide the main stems along a robust pergola. Prune the current season's growth back in summer and reduce spurs in winter for reliable flowering. Long, elegant racemes of pure white pea-flowers open with the emerging foliage in late spring and carry a light fragrance.

planting.note: Plant dormant against a strong structure in spring.

directions: Position the plant at nursery depth and train it up a durable support. Twice-yearly pruning, once in summer and once in winter, keeps growth in check and boosts bloom. Deep violet-purple racemes with a rich scent open in late spring as the leaves appear.

planting.note: Double-flowered form; plant dormant on a heavy support.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the pot and secure stems to a strong frame. This selection carries fully double, rosette-like flowers in the deepest violet-purple, so the trusses look dense and plush. Prune in summer and winter to maintain spurs; bloom comes in late spring with the new leaves.

planting.note: Sometimes sold as 'Pink Ice'; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and train onto a sturdy overhead support. Prune whippy shoots in midsummer and cut spurs back in winter. Long racemes of rosy-pink flowers, deeper in the keel and paler at the edges, open with the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant on a strong frame in early spring.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and tie the leader to a heavy support as it climbs. Reduce summer growth to short spurs and prune again in winter for good flowering. Soft blue-lilac racemes with a pleasant scent open with the leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Sometimes sold as 'Lipstick'; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Plant at pot depth and train along a strong horizontal beam. This bicolor selection bears pale mauve-white flowers brushed with deeper pink toward the tips, giving a soft two-tone effect. Prune twice a year to keep spurs productive; flowers open in late spring with the emerging leaves.

planting.note: Free-flowering 'Issai' type that blooms young; plant in spring.

directions: Set out at nursery depth against a sturdy support; this compact, free-flowering type often blooms within a year or two of planting. Prune lightly in summer and winter to shape. Dense trusses of dusky violet-blue flowers open in late spring, with a good chance of scattered later blooms.

planting.note: Plant dormant against a very strong support in spring.

directions: Plant at pot depth beside a heavy pergola or masonry frame that can bear the twining stems. Prune vigorous shoots back in summer and again in winter to control the plant and build spurs. Fragrant pure-white racemes open in mid to late spring, mostly ahead of the foliage.

planting.note: Reliable early bloomer; plant dormant on a strong frame.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and anchor the leader to a durable support; this selection is noted for flowering freely and fairly young. Prune in summer and winter to keep it in bounds. Masses of blue-violet racemes open in late spring, often followed by a lighter second flush.

planting.note: Strongly scented selection; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and train onto a sturdy support. Cut back long summer shoots and reduce the spurs again in winter to encourage bloom. Richly fragrant rosy-purple racemes open in late spring, prized for their strong sweet scent.

planting.note: Repeat-blooming selection; plant dormant on a strong support.

directions: Plant at pot depth and secure the stems to a heavy frame. This selection tends to flower young and often reblooms lightly through summer, so prune only enough to shape and control it. Deep purple racemes open in late spring with the main show, followed by scattered later trusses.

planting.note: Early, free-flowering selection; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Plant at nursery depth beside a strong support and tie in the leaders as they climb. This selection is valued for flowering early in the season and while still young. Fragrant deep blue-purple racemes open in mid to late spring, generally before the leaves are fully out.

planting.note: Compact, quick to bloom; good for arbors and large containers.

directions: Plant at pot depth on a trellis, post, or large container; this restrained native usually flowers in its first or second year. Trim after the main flush to keep it neat and prompt repeat bloom. Compact lavender-purple clusters appear in late spring and often flush again through summer.

planting.note: White-flowered native selection; plant in spring.

directions: Set out at nursery depth on a sturdy trellis or post in moist, fertile soil. This well-mannered native carries dense clusters of pure white flowers and stays far smaller than the Asian wisterias. Prune after flowering to shape; the first bloom comes in early summer with a chance of later flushes.

planting.note: Deep-colored native selection; plant in spring.

directions: Plant at pot depth against a trellis or arbor; like other native wisterias it is easy to keep in scale. Its clusters are a notably deep violet-purple and appear once the foliage is out. Prune lightly after the main show to encourage repeat bloom through the warmer months.

planting.note: Extremely cold hardy and reblooming; plant in spring.

directions: Plant at nursery depth beside a strong support; this exceptionally hardy selection survives hard winters and often blooms the first summer after planting. It can flower two or three times in a season, so prune only to shape. Fragrant silvery-blue racemes open in early summer and repeat into late summer.

planting.note: Cold-hardy, reliable bloomer; plant in spring.

directions: Set out at pot depth on a sturdy support in cold-winter gardens where Asian wisterias struggle. It blooms young and dependably, so light shaping is all the pruning it needs. Long lilac-purple racemes open in early summer, a little later than the Asian species.

planting.note: Hardy white-flowered native selection; plant in spring.

directions: Plant at nursery depth against a strong support in a cold-climate garden. This selection carries fragrant white racemes and shares the species' good winter hardiness and young flowering habit. Blooms open in early summer, with occasional later trusses.

planting.note: Fragrant white silky wisteria; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Plant at pot depth and train the stems along a durable frame. The short, plump white racemes are cloaked in silvery hairs and often show a small yellow blotch, with a strong sweet scent. Prune hard in summer and tidy in winter; flowers open with the new leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Violet silky wisteria; plant dormant on a strong support.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and guide the stems onto a sturdy pergola. The short racemes are violet-purple and covered in fine silky hairs, carrying the strong fragrance typical of silky wisteria. Prune whippy growth in summer and again in winter; bloom comes with the leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Early, free-flowering silky wisteria; plant dormant in spring.

directions: Plant at nursery depth and train along a strong support. This selection tends to flower early and freely, with short violet racemes that are pale toward the base and scented. Prune in summer and winter to build spurs; flowers open with the emerging foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost; the vine dies back and resprouts from roots each spring.

directions: Sow scarified seed or set transplants where the roots have room to spread, as this native vine suckers freely. Give it a fence or arbor to scramble across and cut spent stems to the ground in late winter. Fringed lavender flowers appear all summer and are followed by egg-shaped edible maypop fruits.

planting.note: Plant only in warm, frost-free weather or grow in a large container to overwinter indoors.

directions: Provide rich, consistently moist soil and a strong trellis for this vigorous tropical climber. In cooler zones grow it in a pot and move it under cover before temperatures fall below 50 degrees. Brilliant scarlet flowers with a purple-and-white crown appear over a long warm season.

planting.note: Plant where its vigorous growth can be contained, as it can become invasive in mild climates.

directions: Give this fast climber a large support and prune hard to keep it in bounds. The pendant pink flowers hang like lanterns and are followed by elongated golden banana-shaped fruit. Avoid planting near natural areas where it can escape and smother native vegetation.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost against a warm trellis or fence.

directions: Plant this free-flowering hybrid against a sunny support and keep the roots evenly moist through summer. Deep violet-purple flowers with recurved petals open in profusion from midsummer into autumn. Propagate from softwood cuttings, as this named clone does not come true from seed.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather or grow in a container to overwinter frost-free.

directions: Grow this coccinea-and-incarnata hybrid on a robust trellis in rich, moist soil. Striking deep-red flowers with a white-centered crown appear continuously in warm weather. Take cuttings to preserve the cultivar, which is grown from vegetative propagation.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this hardy hybrid can resprout from roots after a cold winter.

directions: Set this vigorous hardy hybrid against a fence or arbor where its fragrance can be enjoyed. Large fringed lavender flowers are notably sweet-scented and appear all summer. Prune back winter-damaged stems in spring, as new growth flowers on the current season's wood.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free conditions or keep in a greenhouse over winter.

directions: Recognizable by its distinctly four-winged stems, this vine needs warmth, humidity and a strong support. Crimson flowers with a banded purple-and-white crown are richly fragrant and give way to edible fruit. Keep the soil moist and feed regularly during active growth.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall after the danger of frost passes.

directions: Train this caerulea-type selection up a trellis in full sun and free-draining soil. Pale blue-and-white star-shaped flowers appear over a long summer season. Propagate from cuttings to keep the form true and mulch the base in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered, sunny spot with support.

directions: This ivory-white form of the blue passionflower climbs vigorously to 15 feet on a fence or arbor. The pure white fragrant flowers open all summer and stand out beautifully against dark foliage. Cut back hard in spring and mulch the crown where winters are cold.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a warm, sunny support.

directions: A vigorous free-flowering hybrid bearing broad deep-violet flowers over glossy foliage. Train it up a trellis in full sun and keep the roots cool with mulch. Blooms appear from early summer through autumn; propagate from cuttings to keep the color true.

planting.note: Plant in a sunny sheltered position after the last frost.

directions: This award-winning hybrid produces vivid purple-blue flowers freely over a long season. Grow it up a trellis or through a shrub in full sun with well-drained soil. It is more cold-tolerant than many hybrids and regrows from the base after mild frosts.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered site with support.

directions: A large white-flowered caerulea selection that climbs vigorously up walls and fences. The pure white fragrant blooms appear throughout summer and into fall. Provide a sturdy support and mulch the base to protect roots in cold winters.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a warm trellis or wall.

directions: Grown for its clear sky-blue flowers with a distinctive banded crown. Train up a support in full sun and keep evenly watered through the growing season. Blooms open in succession from summer to autumn; take cuttings to propagate.

planting.note: Plant only in tropical, frost-free gardens on a strong support.

directions: This tropical climber has glossy laurel-like leaves and fragrant red-and-white banded flowers. Give it warmth, humidity and a robust trellis to scramble over. Flowers are followed by egg-shaped yellow edible fruit in the tropics.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather or grow in a container to move indoors in winter.

directions: A large-flowered hybrid bearing fragrant lavender-purple blooms up to five inches across. Grow on a strong support in rich, moist soil and full sun. Flowers appear continuously in warm weather and are followed by rounded edible fruit.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny sheltered spot with support.

directions: A compact, free-flowering hybrid with deep violet-blue flowers, well suited to container growing. Train up a small obelisk or trellis and keep the soil evenly moist. It blooms prolifically through summer and can be overwintered under cover in colder areas.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny position with sturdy support.

directions: A robust hybrid producing large blue-purple flowers over a long summer season. Train up a fence or trellis in full sun with free-draining soil. Prune in spring to shape and propagate from softwood cuttings.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a warm support in full sun.

directions: This hybrid bears nodding deep-purple flowers with long recurved sepals. Grow it up a trellis in full sun and keep the roots consistently moist. Flowering continues from midsummer to fall; take cuttings to preserve the cultivar.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, sheltered site.

directions: A vigorous free-flowering hybrid with rich purple flowers over a long season. Provide a trellis or arbor in full sun and mulch to keep roots cool. Blooms from early summer into autumn and regrows from the base after light frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sunny position with support.

directions: This selection is prized for its clean white flowers with a pale central crown. Grow it up a trellis in full sun and keep evenly watered in summer. It blooms freely through the warm months; propagate from cuttings to keep it true.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather; self-seeds readily in frost-free gardens.

directions: A slender native vine grown as a larval host for fritillary and other butterflies. Its tiny greenish flowers are followed by small dark-purple berries. It tolerates partial shade and its older stems develop the characteristic corky bark.

planting.note: Grow in a container in most climates and overwinter under warm cover.

directions: A compact hybrid bearing star-shaped golden-yellow flowers over mottled foliage. Well suited to pots and hanging baskets in bright warm conditions. Keep it above 50 degrees and propagate from cuttings to maintain the form.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sunny sheltered site with support.

directions: A more compact caerulea-type hybrid with blue-and-white flowers, good for smaller supports. Train up an obelisk or trellis in full sun and keep evenly moist. It flowers prolifically through summer and can be grown in a large container.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free warmth or grow under glass in cooler regions.

directions: Named for its grape-like leaves, this vine bears brilliant scarlet flowers with narrow petals. Give it warmth, humidity and a strong support to climb. Keep the soil moist and feed during active growth for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Grow in a greenhouse or frost-free garden with strong support.

directions: Distinctive for producing its bright red flowers in long hanging clusters rather than singly. This tropical climber needs warmth, humidity and a robust trellis. It is a classic greenhouse plant and parent to many showy hybrids.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sunny sheltered site.

directions: A refined hybrid with soft pastel lavender-and-cream flowers over a long season. Train up a trellis in full sun with free-draining soil. It flowers freely through summer and is propagated from cuttings to keep the delicate coloring.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered spot; tolerates light frost when established.

directions: This hardier species bears fragrant flowers with a striking banded crown resembling a sea anemone. Grow it up a trellis in full sun to partial shade with moist, well-drained soil. It flowers from spring into summer and is among the more cold-tolerant passionflowers.

planting.note: Grow in frost-free conditions or a cool greenhouse with support.

directions: This Andean species bears vivid scarlet flowers with a short blue-and-white crown. It favors cooler tropical highlands and a bright, airy position. Provide a sturdy trellis and keep the soil moist during active growth.

planting.note: Plant where its vigor can be contained, as it spreads aggressively in mild climates.

directions: This fast highland vine has soft downy leaves and pendant pink tubular flowers. Give it a large support and prune firmly to control its rampant growth. Elongated yellow banana-like fruit follows; avoid planting near wild areas where it can naturalize.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather on a strong support or grow in a large pot.

directions: Grown as much for its intricate white-and-purple crowned flowers as for its fruit. Provide a sturdy fence or trellis, rich soil and regular water and feeding. The fragrant flowers give way to the familiar wrinkled purple edible passion fruit.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather or grow in a container to overwinter frost-free.

directions: An old, fragrant hybrid of alata and caerulea with large pink-and-purple crowned flowers. Grow it on a strong support in rich, moist soil and full sun. It is very free-flowering in warmth and propagated from cuttings.

planting.note: Grow in warm, frost-free conditions, often as a foliage container plant.

directions: Grown chiefly for its striking foliage, with three silvery bands running along each leaf. The small cream flowers are lightly fragrant and secondary to the leaf display. Give it warmth, bright indirect light and a small trellis in a pot.

planting.note: Set out young plants after the last spring frost against a sturdy support.

directions: Cold-stratify seed for a couple of months, then sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil and expect uneven sprouting. Give the vine a heavy trellis, arbor, or wall to grip with its aerial rootlets, and cut it back hard each dormant season to control its spread. Expect the first orange-red trumpets in mid to late summer once the plant is a few years established.

planting.note: Plant after frost where its clear yellow flowers can be seen from below.

directions: This yellow-flowered form comes true only from cuttings or layering, so root softwood cuttings in early summer rather than relying on seed. Train the shoots onto a solid frame and prune in late winter to keep the tangle in bounds. Butter-yellow trumpets open across summer and draw hummingbirds all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost against a wall or pergola in full sun.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and provide strong support before growth takes off. Water through the first two summers, then let it fend for itself once rooted. Rich scarlet-red trumpets appear from midsummer into autumn on the current season's growth, so prune in early spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once the ground has warmed and frost has passed.

directions: Give this vigorous vine a robust support such as a metal arch or thick wire, as it grows fast and heavy. Cut back to a low framework each late winter to encourage a flush of bloom-bearing new wood. Deep fiery orange-red trumpets crowd the tips from July until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm sunny spot with room to climb.

directions: Position against a sun-facing wall and tie in the leading shoots until the plant grips on its own. Keep it in check with a hard spring cut, removing weak and crowded stems. Golden-yellow trumpets flushed with an orange throat open through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost where the deep flowers show against a light wall.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and mulch the base to conserve moisture through establishment. Train onto a sturdy support and shorten side shoots to a few buds each spring. Dark red-purple trumpets appear from midsummer and continue until the first frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring for an earlier-than-usual run of bloom.

directions: Set this selection against a warm support and keep it watered while young roots take hold. Prune to a permanent framework in late winter to steer strong flowering growth. Prized for opening its scarlet-orange trumpets earlier in the season than the common form.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a fence or trellis in full sun.

directions: Give this cultivar a strong structure and full sun for the heaviest bloom. Cut it back near ground level each spring to renew the flowering wood and limit its reach. Warm reddish-orange trumpets cluster at the shoot tips from summer into fall, feeding hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant in a warm, sheltered position in spring after the frosts.

directions: Because this vine holds on loosely, fasten the shoots to a support until it is well established. Prune in early spring and shelter the base from harsh winter cold. Wide peach-and-apricot trumpets with contrasting throats open generously across summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with a strong support in place.

directions: This classic hybrid combines vigor with abundant bloom, so give it a heavy-duty arch, pergola, or wall. Water while establishing and prune to a low framework each late winter for the best flowering. Salmon-red trumpets appear in large clusters from midsummer until autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm sunny spot against a support.

directions: Set out in spring and tie in the leaders until the vine grips on its own. Keep it tidy with a firm cut in early spring, taking stems back to a permanent framework. Blends of apricot, orange, and red glow in the trumpets from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant this compact form in spring on a smaller trellis or obelisk.

directions: This tamer selection suits a large container or a modest trellis rather than a whole wall. Prune lightly in early spring and water during dry spells. Red-orange trumpets bloom on the new growth from midsummer through early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun on a compact support.

directions: A restrained grower that fits smaller gardens and patio containers. Give it full sun and a light spring prune to shape and encourage bloom. Warm golden-yellow trumpets open across summer and into early autumn.
